compounds.realWorldUse: Drinking, cleaning, cooling everything
compounds.funFact: Water is the only substance that naturally exists as solid, liquid and gas on Earth. Your body is about 60% water!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fizzy drinks, fire extinguishers, dry ice
compounds.funFact: CO₂ is what makes fizzy drinks bubbly. In high concentrations it can make you pass out!

compounds.realWorldUse: Food seasoning, preserving food, de-icing roads
compounds.funFact: Salt is made from two poisons — sodium metal and chlorine gas — yet together they're safe to eat. Chemistry is wild!

compounds.realWorldUse: Breathing, hospitals, rocket fuel
compounds.funFact: Pure oxygen makes things burn far faster — even materials that don't normally burn in air can ignite in pure oxygen!

compounds.realWorldUse: Rocket fuel, fuel cells, making ammonia
compounds.funFact: Hydrogen is the lightest element and most abundant in the universe. The Hindenburg disaster happened because it was filled with hydrogen!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertilisers, cleaning products, refrigeration
compounds.funFact: Ammonia smells like very strong wee! Your body makes small amounts when breaking down protein.

compounds.realWorldUse: Natural gas for cooking and heating
compounds.funFact: Cows produce huge amounts of methane when they burp and fart — farming contributes significantly to climate change!

compounds.realWorldUse: Bleaching hair, disinfecting wounds, rocket propellant
compounds.funFact: Concentrated hydrogen peroxide is rocket fuel! The pharmacy version is only 3% — rocket grade is 90%+.

compounds.realWorldUse: Water purification (tiny amounts), making PVC plastic
compounds.funFact: Chlorine gas was used as a chemical weapon in WW1. Even tiny amounts can destroy your lungs!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — chlorine gas is a deadly poison used as a weapon in WW1!

compounds.realWorldUse: Your stomach makes it to digest food!
compounds.funFact: Your stomach acid is strong enough to dissolve metal. Your stomach lining replaces itself every few days to survive!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — concentrated HCl burns through skin and metal!

compounds.realWorldUse: Food packaging, liquid nitrogen for freezing
compounds.funFact: 78% of the air you breathe is nitrogen! Liquid nitrogen at -196°C instantly freezes anything it touches.

compounds.realWorldUse: Byproduct of incomplete combustion — not useful, just deadly
compounds.funFact: CO is the silent killer — no smell, no colour. It kills by stopping your blood carrying oxygen.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — carbon monoxide is odourless and kills silently!

compounds.realWorldUse: Found in volcanoes and swamps
compounds.funFact: H₂S smells of rotten eggs. At high concentrations it paralyses your sense of smell so you can't detect it — then it kills you!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — hydrogen sulfide is extremely toxic and smells of rotten eggs!

compounds.realWorldUse: Food preservative in tiny amounts, making sulfuric acid
compounds.funFact: Volcanoes release massive amounts of SO₂. Large eruptions can cool the entire planet by blocking sunlight!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — SO₂ causes acid rain and serious lung damage!

compounds.realWorldUse: Red pigment in paint, thermite reactions
compounds.funFact: Mars is red because its surface is covered in iron oxide — rust! The whole planet is one giant rusty rock.

compounds.realWorldUse: Building material, chalk, cement, antacid tablets
compounds.funFact: The White Cliffs of Dover are calcium carbonate — billions of tiny shells from sea creatures that lived 70 million years ago!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making cement, treating soil, water purification
compounds.funFact: Adding water to quicklime gets hot enough to boil water! Romans used this reaction as an incendiary weapon.

compounds.realWorldUse: Glass, computer chips, beach sand, quartz watches
compounds.funFact: Window glass, beach sand, and the chips in your computer are all silicon dioxide. It's literally everywhere!

compounds.realWorldUse: Antacid tablets, fireproofing, cement
compounds.funFact: Burning magnesium produces blinding white light and magnesium oxide. Photographers used it as a flash before electric lights!

compounds.realWorldUse: Rubies and sapphires are aluminium oxide with trace impurities!
compounds.funFact: Rubies and sapphires are the same compound — just with different impurities giving them their colour. Gemstones are fancy chemistry!

compounds.realWorldUse: Anaesthetic, whipped cream dispensers, racing car boost
compounds.funFact: Laughing gas makes people giggle uncontrollably. It also powers whipped cream canisters and boosts race car engines!

compounds.realWorldUse: Makes smog, used in making nitric acid
compounds.funFact: NO₂ is the brown smog over cities. Diesel engines produce it — that's why cities are banning diesel!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — nitrogen dioxide is a toxic brown gas that causes acid rain and lung damage!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making Teflon, toothpaste fluoride
compounds.funFact: Fluorine burns through glass, concrete, and even water. Early scientists trying to isolate it were killed or blinded!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — fluorine reacts violently with almost everything, including water!

compounds.realWorldUse: Etching glass, making semiconductors
compounds.funFact: HF passes through skin painlessly then dissolves your bones from inside. Even tiny amounts can be fatal!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — hydrofluoric acid dissolves bones from inside. Extremely deadly!

compounds.realWorldUse: Ozone layer, water purification
compounds.funFact: The ozone layer is just oxygen in groups of three instead of two. Without it, UV radiation would make life on Earth impossible!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fumigant for stored grain
compounds.funFact: Phosphine was detected in Venus's atmosphere in 2020, causing excitement about possible alien life! It also causes will-o'-the-wisps in marshes.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — phosphine is an extremely toxic gas that can ignite spontaneously!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fool's Gold (pyrite) is iron sulfide!
compounds.funFact: Fool's Gold famously tricked gold prospectors during the Gold Rush. It's iron sulfide and worth almost nothing!

compounds.realWorldUse: Killing algae in swimming pools, fungicide for crops
compounds.funFact: Copper sulfate crystals are a stunning bright blue. Growing them is a classic school chemistry experiment!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making soap, unblocking drains, making paper
compounds.funFact: Soap is made by reacting fats with lye (NaOH). The lye itself is caustic, but the reaction turns it into something gentle enough to wash with!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — sodium hydroxide dissolves skin and causes severe chemical burns!

compounds.realWorldUse: Baking, antacid, fire extinguishers, cleaning
compounds.funFact: Baking soda and vinegar react to make CO₂, making cakes rise. This same reaction powers model volcano experiments!

compounds.realWorldUse: Colouring glass ruby red, electronics
compounds.funFact: Gold oxide decomposes back to pure gold when heated — gold is so unreactive it doesn't even like being an oxide!

compounds.realWorldUse: Button batteries, antimicrobial coatings
compounds.funFact: Silver oxide powers tiny batteries in watches and hearing aids. Silver has natural antimicrobial properties — that's why we used silver cutlery!

compounds.realWorldUse: Sunscreen, nappy cream, white paint, rubber
compounds.funFact: Zinc oxide is the white stuff lifeguards put on their noses! It blocks UV rays completely — it's in the best sunscreens.

compounds.realWorldUse: White paint, sunscreen, food whitener
compounds.funFact: Almost all white paint contains titanium dioxide. It's also in most white sweets. You've definitely eaten it!

compounds.realWorldUse: Pigment in ceramics and glass
compounds.funFact: The Statue of Liberty is green because copper reacts with air and water. When new, it was shiny brown like a copper coin!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making fertilisers and explosives like TNT
compounds.funFact: Nitric acid turns skin bright yellow — a chemical reaction with proteins. It's used to make most explosives and fertilisers!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — nitric acid causes severe burns and turns your skin bright yellow!

compounds.realWorldUse: Car batteries, fertilisers — most produced industrial chemical
compounds.funFact: Sulfuric acid is the world's most industrially produced chemical. It violently dehydrates organic matter — it can turn sugar to carbon instantly!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — sulfuric acid boils water on contact, causes catastrophic burns, and dissolves metals!

compounds.realWorldUse: Hydrogen bombs, hydrogen source for spacecraft
compounds.funFact: Lithium hydride is used in thermonuclear (hydrogen) bombs! It reacts with neutrons to produce the fuel for fusion.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — lithium hydride explodes violently with water and is used in nuclear weapons!

compounds.realWorldUse: Water purification, treating fungal infections
compounds.funFact: A single crystal dropped into water creates a stunning swirling purple cloud. It's one of the most dramatic colour reactions in chemistry!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertiliser (most common), mining explosive
compounds.funFact: The 2020 Beirut explosion was caused by 2,750 tonnes of ammonium nitrate. It was the most powerful non-nuclear explosion in recent history!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — ammonium nitrate caused the catastrophic 2020 Beirut explosion!

compounds.realWorldUse: Flame retardants, water treatment
compounds.funFact: Bromine is one of only two elements liquid at room temperature (the other is mercury). It's dark red and evaporates into toxic vapour!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — bromine vapour causes severe chemical burns to skin, eyes and lungs!

compounds.realWorldUse: Antiseptic, thyroid medicine, water purification
compounds.funFact: Iodine turns starch deep blue-black — the classic test for starch in biology! Iodine molecules get trapped inside the starch spiral.

compounds.realWorldUse: Household bleach, water purification, swimming pools
compounds.funFact: Never mix bleach with ammonia or vinegar — it creates toxic chloramine gas! This is one of the most common household chemical accidents.

compounds.realWorldUse: Safety matches, fireworks, oxygen generation
compounds.funFact: Match heads contain potassium chlorate. When heated it releases pure oxygen which ignites. Mixed with sugar it makes a dangerous explosive!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium chlorate mixed with any fuel becomes a shock-sensitive explosive!

compounds.realWorldUse: Car airbags — it explodes to inflate them!
compounds.funFact: Your car airbag works by exploding sodium azide! The explosion happens in milliseconds, inflating the bag before your face hits the wheel.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — sodium azide is a deadly poison AND explosive. Even tiny amounts are fatal!

compounds.realWorldUse: Yellow pigment, making tungsten for lightbulb filaments
compounds.funFact: Tungsten has the highest melting point of all metals at 3422°C. Lightbulb filaments are tungsten — they glow white-hot without melting!

compounds.realWorldUse: Humidity indicator cards, blue pigment
compounds.funFact: Cobalt chloride changes from blue when dry to pink when wet — used in weather cards and 'magic' ink! Amazing colour chemistry.

compounds.realWorldUse: Pepto-Bismol stomach medicine, yellow pigment
compounds.funFact: Pepto-Bismol contains bismuth compounds! Bismuth crystals form spectacular rainbow-coloured geometric structures.

compounds.realWorldUse: Washing powder, making glass, softening hard water
compounds.funFact: Ancient Egyptians extracted sodium carbonate from burnt seaweed to make glass and soap. It's still one of the most used industrial chemicals!

compounds.realWorldUse: Cola drinks (gives the tangy taste!), rust removal, fertiliser
compounds.funFact: That tangy taste in Coca-Cola comes from phosphoric acid! It's also an effective rust remover — it reacts with iron oxide and dissolves it.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making lenses for telescopes and cameras
compounds.funFact: Fluorite glows bright blue under UV light — the word 'fluorescence' was named after this mineral! Some specimens are spectacularly colourful.

compounds.realWorldUse: De-icing roads, food firming agent, drying agent
compounds.funFact: Calcium chloride absorbs so much water from the air it can turn into a liquid puddle just sitting out! It melts ice at -30°C.

compounds.realWorldUse: Food additive, de-icing, making tofu firm
compounds.funFact: Magnesium chloride is what makes tofu firm! It's also found in seawater — when seawater evaporates it leaves behind this and other salts.

compounds.realWorldUse: Medical X-ray contrast, white paint pigment
compounds.funFact: Patients drink a 'barium meal' before X-rays — it shows up bright white and lets doctors see inside your digestive system. It's completely harmless!

compounds.realWorldUse: Bath salts for sore muscles, growing vegetables
compounds.funFact: Epsom salts were discovered in Epsom, Surrey in 1618 when a farmer noticed his cows wouldn't drink from a bitter pool — but wounds healed faster when bathed in it!

compounds.realWorldUse: Plaster of Paris, plasterboard walls, food additive
compounds.funFact: Plaster of Paris is named after gypsum deposits in Paris. Your walls are probably lined with it!

compounds.realWorldUse: Gunpowder ingredient, food preservative, fertiliser
compounds.funFact: Saltpetre is one of the three ingredients of gunpowder, known since ancient China. It was so valuable people collected it from dunghills!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium nitrate is a component of gunpowder and can cause fires and explosions!

compounds.realWorldUse: Used historically to make pure mercury and oxygen
compounds.funFact: Heating mercury oxide decomposes it into liquid mercury and oxygen gas. This is how oxygen was first discovered in 1774!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — mercury is an extremely toxic heavy metal that damages the brain and nervous system!

compounds.realWorldUse: Etching circuit boards, water treatment
compounds.funFact: Iron chloride etches the copper circuit boards inside every electronic device. Without it we couldn't make smartphones or computers!

compounds.realWorldUse: Salt substitute, fertiliser
compounds.funFact: Potassium chloride is used as a low-sodium salt substitute. In very high doses it stops the heart — used controversially in lethal injections.

compounds.realWorldUse: Cutting tools, sandpaper, bulletproof vests
compounds.funFact: Silicon carbide is almost as hard as diamond! Naturally occurring SiC was first found in a meteor crater. Lab-grown moissanite is used in jewellery.

compounds.realWorldUse: Lubricant, cosmetics, bulletproof materials
compounds.funFact: Cubic boron nitride is the second hardest material after diamond! It cuts steel that diamond can't handle.

compounds.realWorldUse: The hard component of steel — makes it strong
compounds.funFact: Steel is iron with a tiny amount of carbon (0.2-2.1%). The carbon forms iron carbide crystals that make steel far stronger than pure iron.

compounds.realWorldUse: Photographic film, photochromic glasses
compounds.funFact: Old photographic film contains silver chloride — it darkens when light hits it, recording the image. Digital cameras made it mostly obsolete.

compounds.realWorldUse: Electrical insulation in power stations, eye surgery
compounds.funFact: SF₆ makes your voice go very deep — the opposite of helium! It's 5x denser than air so you can float a paper boat on invisible SF₆.

compounds.realWorldUse: Etching silicon chips, making semiconductors
compounds.funFact: Xenon was thought completely unreactive — noble gases don't react, right? Wrong! XeF₂ shocked the scientific world in 1962!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — xenon difluoride reacts explosively with water!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertiliser (50% of world nitrogen fertiliser!), skincare
compounds.funFact: Urea was the first organic compound synthesised in a lab (1828), proving 'life chemicals' can be made artificially. It's found naturally in urine!

compounds.realWorldUse: Antiseptic eyewash, ant killer, Pyrex glass
compounds.funFact: Pyrex glass (used for oven dishes and lab equipment) contains boric acid. That's why it doesn't crack going from hot to cold!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making liquid soap, batteries, food processing
compounds.funFact: Potassium hydroxide makes soft liquid soaps while sodium hydroxide makes hard bar soaps. Both are caustic until they react to form soap!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium hydroxide causes severe chemical burns!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making cement and plaster, treating acidic soil
compounds.funFact: Breathe CO₂ into limewater (calcium hydroxide solution) and it goes milky white — the classic test for carbon dioxide in school science!

compounds.realWorldUse: Soldering flux, fertiliser, cough medicine, salty liquorice!
compounds.funFact: Ammonium chloride is what makes Scandinavian salty liquorice taste intensely salty and weird. It's also used to help solder metal joints!

compounds.realWorldUse: Table salt additive, nuclear emergency tablets
compounds.funFact: After nuclear accidents, people take potassium iodide tablets to protect their thyroid gland from radioactive iodine!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making patina on copper and bronze artworks, pyrotechnics
compounds.funFact: Copper nitrate artificially ages bronze sculptures — it creates the green patina that usually takes decades to form naturally!

compounds.realWorldUse: Red fireworks and flares, sensitive toothpaste
compounds.funFact: Strontium chloride makes brilliant crimson-red fireworks! It's also in sensitive toothpaste — strontium ions block tiny tubules in sensitive teeth.

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertiliser, food preservative in cured meats
compounds.funFact: Sodium nitrate was so valuable in the 19th century that Chile and Bolivia fought a war over it! It's still used to preserve bacon and salami.

compounds.realWorldUse: Green pigment in paint, polishing compound
compounds.funFact: Chromium oxide gives emeralds their green colour! The same compound is used to polish metals to a mirror finish.

compounds.realWorldUse: Inside batteries, making glass, bleaching
compounds.funFact: Manganese dioxide is inside most disposable batteries! It acts as a catalyst, rapidly breaking down hydrogen peroxide into water and oxygen.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making lead crystal glass, car batteries
compounds.funFact: Ancient Romans used lead compounds as a sweetener in wine — this may have contributed to the fall of the Roman Empire through mass lead poisoning!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — lead compounds cause brain damage, especially in children!

compounds.realWorldUse: Nuclear reactor fuel — powers ~10% of world electricity
compounds.funFact: One kilogram of uranium fuel produces as much energy as 45,000 kg of coal! Nuclear fuel pellets are fingertip-sized but contain enormous energy.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — uranium dioxide is radioactive and causes radiation sickness!

compounds.realWorldUse: Cleaning semiconductor chambers, making LCD screens
compounds.funFact: NF₃ is 17,000 times more potent a greenhouse gas than CO₂! Ironically it's used to make 'green' solar panels and LCD screens.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — nitrogen trifluoride is a powerful oxidiser and toxic gas!

compounds.realWorldUse: Iron supplements, lawn treatments, making ink
compounds.funFact: Medieval monks made iron gall ink from ferrous sulfate to write manuscripts. The Magna Carta was written in iron gall ink!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making glass, soap, cocoa processing
compounds.funFact: Potash was made by burning wood and leaching ashes — that's why it's called POTash! It was one of North America's first exports.

compounds.realWorldUse: Purifying drinking water, making paper, antiperspirant
compounds.funFact: Aluminium sulfate clumps dirt particles in water together so they sink — flocculation, used in every water treatment plant!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making sulfuric acid, large-scale energy storage batteries
compounds.funFact: Vanadium pentoxide is the key catalyst for making sulfuric acid — the world's most produced industrial chemical!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — vanadium pentoxide causes severe lung damage and kidney failure!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fireproofing wood, preserving eggs, making silica gel
compounds.funFact: Sodium silicate solution (water glass) was used to preserve eggs for months before refrigerators! Drop it in acid and it forms beautiful chemical garden structures.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making plastics and pharmaceuticals (in industry, carefully)
compounds.funFact: HCN smells like almonds and acts within seconds — it stops cells using oxygen. Apple seeds contain tiny amounts of a compound that releases it!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — hydrogen cyanide is one of the most lethal poisons known. Tiny amounts cause almost instant death!

compounds.realWorldUse: Historically rat poison; now treats some leukaemias
compounds.funFact: Arsenic trioxide was the Borgias' favourite poison in Renaissance Italy! Incredibly, tiny doses now treat a specific type of leukaemia.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — arsenic trioxide is a deadly poison historically used for murder!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making viscose rayon fabric, dissolving rubber
compounds.funFact: Carbon disulfide ignites at only 90°C — well below boiling water! It can catch fire just from a warm lightbulb.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — extremely flammable (ignites at 90°C) and neurotoxic!

compounds.realWorldUse: Photography, leather tanning, orange fireworks
compounds.funFact: The 'chemical volcano' demonstration uses potassium dichromate — it puffs up into a giant wormy mass when ignited!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium dichromate is a carcinogen and powerful oxidiser that causes cancer!

compounds.realWorldUse: Oxygen source in submarines, bleaching
compounds.funFact: Sodium peroxide is used in submarine emergency breathing! When water or CO₂ touches it, it releases pure oxygen.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — sodium peroxide reacts violently and explosively with water!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making special glasses for telescopes
compounds.funFact: Lithium compounds treat bipolar disorder. Lithium batteries in your phone contain lithium compounds storing electrical energy.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making nickel metal, colouring glass green, fuel cells
compounds.funFact: Nickel allergy is one of the most common contact allergies — caused by nickel compounds reacting with sweat on your skin!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making semiconductors, chemical synthesis
compounds.funFact: Diborane ignites spontaneously in air and burns with an eerie green flame. It was considered as rocket fuel for nuclear aircraft in the 1950s!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — diborane ignites spontaneously in air and is highly toxic!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertiliser, concrete accelerator, wastewater treatment
compounds.funFact: Calcium nitrate is called Norwegian saltpetre — first made in Norway using hydroelectric power. An early example of green chemistry!

compounds.realWorldUse: Vinegar, making plastics, food preservation
compounds.funFact: Vinegar is diluted acetic acid made by bacteria fermenting wine or cider. Concentrated glacial acetic acid is actually quite dangerous!

compounds.realWorldUse: Signalling molecule in the body, treating heart disease
compounds.funFact: Your own body makes nitric oxide as a signalling molecule! It dilates blood vessels and was studied for Viagra's development. Nobel Prize winner!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making acetylene for welding
compounds.funFact: Drop calcium carbide in water and it makes acetylene gas — Victorian miners used this reaction in carbide lamps to light coal mines!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — reacts with water to produce explosive acetylene gas!

compounds.realWorldUse: Primary source of copper ore (chalcocite)
compounds.funFact: Most of the world's copper comes from copper sulfide ores! Smelting separates the copper and sulfur using heat.

compounds.realWorldUse: Mordant for dyeing fabric, etching metal
compounds.funFact: Iron nitrate fixes dyes permanently to fabric. Before modern chemistry, natural dyes were unreliable without mordants like this.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making mirrors, food preservative, reducing agent
compounds.funFact: The first proper mirrors were made by coating glass with tin chloride to deposit silver. Before this, people used polished metal or still water!

compounds.realWorldUse: Gold plating, photography, making ruby glass
compounds.funFact: Medieval glassmakers added tiny amounts of gold chloride to make stunning ruby red stained glass — the gold particles scatter light to appear red!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making cisplatin cancer drug, catalyst for fuel cells
compounds.funFact: Cisplatin, derived from platinum chloride, treats testicular cancer with a 99% cure rate — one of medicine's greatest successes!

compounds.realWorldUse: Gold mining (dissolves gold from rock), electroplating
compounds.funFact: KCN dissolves gold — that's how most gold is extracted from rock! Cyanide leaching is controversial due to environmental damage.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium cyanide kills within minutes and has been used in war crimes!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making sulfuric acid (industrial process)
compounds.funFact: SO₃ reacts so violently with water that adding it directly causes explosions of boiling acid. Industrial plants dissolve it in concentrated H₂SO₄ first!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — sulfur trioxide reacts explosively with water to form concentrated sulfuric acid!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making alloys and ceramics, catalyst
compounds.funFact: When magnesium burns in air, it makes both magnesium oxide AND magnesium nitride. The nitride reacts with water to make ammonia!

compounds.realWorldUse: Welding and cutting metal, making plastics
compounds.funFact: Acetylene burns at 3500°C — hot enough to cut through steel like butter! An oxyacetylene torch is one of the hottest things you can make.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — acetylene can explode spontaneously under pressure!

compounds.realWorldUse: Green fireworks and signal flares
compounds.funFact: The brightest green in fireworks comes from barium nitrate. Military flares and tracer ammunition use it too. Also highly toxic!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — barium nitrate is both a toxic heavy metal compound and an explosive oxidiser!

compounds.realWorldUse: Psychiatric medicine for bipolar disorder, lithium batteries
compounds.funFact: Ancient Greeks drank lithium-rich spring water to treat mental illness without knowing why it worked! It's been used for centuries.

compounds.realWorldUse: Fireworks (green colour!), etching circuit boards
compounds.funFact: Copper chloride makes beautiful green flames in fireworks — a staple of professional pyrotechnics!

compounds.realWorldUse: Source of sulfur dioxide, lithium batteries
compounds.funFact: Pyrite (Fool's Gold) can strike sparks when hit with steel — early humans used it to start fires! The name comes from Greek 'pyr' meaning fire.

compounds.realWorldUse: Purifying nickel metal
compounds.funFact: Nickel carbonyl is liquid at room temperature and among the most toxic substances known — more toxic than carbon monoxide!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — nickel carbonyl is one of the most toxic substances known!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making glass, detergents, paper
compounds.funFact: Glauber's salt (sodium sulfate) was the first chemical produced industrially in large quantities, used as a powerful laxative medicine in the 1600s!

compounds.realWorldUse: Your bones and teeth are mainly calcium phosphate!
compounds.funFact: Your skeleton is calcium phosphate! Bones are about 70% calcium phosphate mineral. It's also what makes teeth hard.

compounds.realWorldUse: Strong antiperspirant deodorant, chemical catalyst
compounds.funFact: Aluminium chloride is the active ingredient in the strongest antiperspirant deodorants — it actually blocks sweat glands. Reacts violently with water!

compounds.realWorldUse: Iron supplement tablets, water purification, pigment
compounds.funFact: Ferric hydroxide is rusty orange and forms when iron rusts in water. Your body uses a similar compound to store iron in iron supplement tablets!

compounds.realWorldUse: Cleaning agent, food additive, water softener
compounds.funFact: Sodium phosphate is added to processed cheese to stop oils separating — that's why American cheese melts so perfectly!

compounds.realWorldUse: Drying agent, making phosphoric acid
compounds.funFact: P₂O₅ is one of the most powerful drying agents known — it reacts so violently with water it can extract moisture from other chemicals!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — reacts explosively with water and causes severe burns!

compounds.realWorldUse: Used historically as antiseptic and to treat syphilis
compounds.funFact: Mercuric chloride was given to syphilis patients in the 1800s. The treatment was often as deadly as the disease — 'a night with Venus, a lifetime with Mercury'!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — mercuric chloride causes kidney failure and death!

compounds.realWorldUse: Emergency breathing apparatus for miners and firefighters
compounds.funFact: Potassium superoxide reacts with the CO₂ and water you breathe out to regenerate fresh oxygen — perfect for breathing apparatus in fires!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium superoxide reacts explosively with water and organic materials!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fireworks (bright green), water softening
compounds.funFact: Barium chloride makes brilliant apple-green flames in fireworks. Despite being used in pyrotechnics, it's also a rat poison!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — barium chloride is extremely toxic and has been used as rat poison!

compounds.realWorldUse: Inside car batteries (lead-acid batteries)
compounds.funFact: Lead sulfate forms on car battery plates during discharge. Recharging converts it back to lead and acid — a reversible chemical reaction!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — lead sulfate is a toxic lead compound causing brain damage!

compounds.realWorldUse: Medical imaging, iodine supplement
compounds.funFact: Sodium iodide crystals flash with visible light when hit by gamma rays — used in radiation detectors to see invisible radiation!

compounds.realWorldUse: Advanced batteries, hydrogen storage for fuel cells
compounds.funFact: Lithium is the only metal that reacts with nitrogen gas at room temperature — making it totally unique among all metals!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — lithium nitride reacts explosively with water!

compounds.realWorldUse: Chrome plating metals
compounds.funFact: Chromic acid is carcinogenic — it causes cancer. It was widely used to clean laboratory equipment but is being phased out due to extreme toxicity!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — chromic acid causes cancer and severe oxidising burns!

compounds.realWorldUse: Rocket fuel oxidiser — used in spacecraft and missiles
compounds.funFact: N₂O₄ was used as rocket fuel in the Apollo lunar module! It ignites spontaneously when it touches certain fuels — no spark needed in space.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — dinitrogen tetroxide is a hypergolic rocket propellant that ignites on contact with fuel!

compounds.realWorldUse: Alcoholic drinks, hand sanitiser, fuel
compounds.funFact: Ethanol is the alcohol in beer, wine and spirits. It's also in hand sanitiser and can run car engines. Burns with an almost invisible blue flame!

compounds.realWorldUse: Preserving biological specimens, making resins
compounds.funFact: Formaldehyde is used to preserve dead bodies and specimens in jars. It's also in some furniture and can off-gas into your home!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — formaldehyde is a toxic carcinogen causing cancer and severe respiratory damage!

compounds.realWorldUse: Weedkiller, bleaching paper
compounds.funFact: Sodium chlorate is a powerful oxidiser. Mixed with sugar it makes a dangerous explosive — it's been used in improvised bombs!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — sodium chlorate mixed with organic material becomes a powerful explosive!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making vanadium steel alloys, catalyst
compounds.funFact: Vanadium steel is used in tools and springs — adding a tiny amount of vanadium makes steel much stronger and more elastic!

compounds.realWorldUse: Research chemical, oxygen source for making other compounds
compounds.funFact: XeF₄ was the second noble gas compound ever made — just weeks after XeF₂ in 1962. It reacts explosively with water producing ozone!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — xenon tetrafluoride reacts explosively with water!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making insecticides, flame retardants and medicines
compounds.funFact: PCl₃ was used to make nerve agents in WWII. It reacts violently with water, producing HCl gas. Even moisture in air makes it fume!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — phosphorus trichloride is used in making chemical weapons!

compounds.realWorldUse: Green pigment in glass, making steel
compounds.funFact: FeO gives bottle glass its characteristic green tint. Ancient Romans made green glass using iron compounds from impure sand!

compounds.realWorldUse: Gold and silver mining, fumigant
compounds.funFact: Calcium cyanide releases hydrogen cyanide gas on contact with moisture — even the moisture in air. It was used as a fumigant under the name 'Cyanogas'.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — calcium cyanide releases deadly hydrogen cyanide gas in moist air!

compounds.realWorldUse: Bearings in jet engines, cutting tools, medical implants
compounds.funFact: Silicon nitride is used in jet engine bearings because it's harder than steel but much lighter. It's also used in hip replacement implants!

compounds.realWorldUse: Bulletproof vests, nuclear reactor control rods
compounds.funFact: Boron carbide is the third hardest material known after diamond and cubic boron nitride. It's used in bulletproof vests and stops nuclear reactions!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making other lead compounds, photography, matches
compounds.funFact: Lead nitrate is one of the few lead compounds that dissolves in water — making it especially dangerous as it can contaminate water supplies!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — lead nitrate is both a toxic heavy metal compound and oxidising explosive!

compounds.realWorldUse: Soldering flux, batteries, wood preservation, smoke grenades
compounds.funFact: Zinc chloride smoke grenades produce a thick white smoke used in military operations and for film effects. It makes the smoke seen in many action movies!

compounds.realWorldUse: Antacid, gymnasts' chalk, cosmetics, fire protection
compounds.funFact: The chalk gymnasts, climbers and weightlifters use on their hands is magnesium carbonate! It absorbs sweat to improve grip.

compounds.realWorldUse: Blue-green pigment, making copper compounds
compounds.funFact: Copper carbonate is the main component of the green patina (verdigris) on copper roofs and the Statue of Liberty!

compounds.realWorldUse: Iron ore (siderite), iron supplements
compounds.funFact: Siderite (iron carbonate) is an iron ore that forms beautiful crystal clusters. Some ancient Roman aqueducts were sealed with iron carbonate deposits!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertiliser, food additive, flame retardant
compounds.funFact: Ammonium sulfate is one of the oldest synthetic fertilisers, used since the 1800s. It's also added to bread flour as a yeast nutrient!

compounds.realWorldUse: Zinc supplement tablets, treating zinc deficiency, wood preservative
compounds.funFact: Zinc sulfate was historically called 'white vitriol'. Zinc deficiency affects 2 billion people worldwide — zinc sulfate tablets help treat it!

compounds.realWorldUse: Nickel plating, making batteries, catalyst
compounds.funFact: Most modern electric vehicle batteries contain nickel sulfate. It's one of the fastest growing industrial chemicals due to the EV revolution!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making lithium cobalt oxide for batteries, blue pigment
compounds.funFact: Your phone battery likely contains cobalt processed from cobalt sulfate. Most of the world's cobalt comes from the Democratic Republic of Congo.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making zinc oxide, mordant for dyeing, fertiliser
compounds.funFact: Zinc nitrate is used to make the zinc oxide in sunscreen and nappy rash cream! It decomposes when heated to give pure zinc oxide.

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertiliser, making concentrated nitric acid, pyrotechnics
compounds.funFact: Magnesium nitrate creates dazzling white sparks in fireworks — the same brilliant white you see in magnesium flares and emergency signals!

compounds.realWorldUse: Humidity control, welding flux, making lithium metal
compounds.funFact: Lithium chloride is so good at absorbing water that it's used to control humidity in air conditioning systems and to dry industrial gases!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making barium salts, lubricant additives, cathode ray tubes
compounds.funFact: Old CRT television screens contained barium oxide in the glass to block X-rays produced inside! Modern flat screens don't need it.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — barium oxide reacts violently with water and is highly toxic!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making tin metal, opaque white glaze on ceramics, gas sensor
compounds.funFact: Tin oxide is used in gas sensors in smoke detectors and carbon monoxide alarms — it changes electrical resistance when it detects certain gases!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making other lead compounds, infrared transmitting glass
compounds.funFact: Lead chloride is used to make special infrared-transmitting glass for heat-seeking missile guidance systems and night vision equipment!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — lead chloride is a highly toxic lead compound causing neurological damage!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making bismuth compounds, iridescent pigments in cosmetics
compounds.funFact: Bismuth chloride is used to make the shimmery, iridescent pigments in nail varnish and eye shadow — it creates interference colours like a soap bubble!

compounds.realWorldUse: Cutting tools, drill bits, jewellery, armour-piercing ammunition
compounds.funFact: Tungsten carbide drill bits can cut through almost anything. It's also used in 'tungsten' wedding rings — actually tungsten carbide, much harder than gold!

compounds.realWorldUse: Green pigment in glass, making fertilisers, steel additive
compounds.funFact: Ancient Egyptians used manganese oxide to decolourise glass and make it crystal clear. Without it, all glass would have a green tint!

compounds.realWorldUse: Chrome plating, mordant for dyeing, catalyst
compounds.funFact: Chromium chloride gives the chrome plating on car parts! The shiny silver finish on taps, door handles and bumpers is chromium deposited from solutions of this compound.

compounds.realWorldUse: Blue pigment in glass and ceramics, making lithium batteries
compounds.funFact: Cobalt oxide is responsible for the stunning cobalt blue colour in medieval stained glass windows and Ming dynasty blue and white porcelain!

compounds.realWorldUse: Toothpaste, water fluoridation, rat poison
compounds.funFact: The fluoride in your toothpaste is sodium fluoride! In tiny amounts it strengthens tooth enamel. In larger amounts it's used as rat poison.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making fluorine gas, etching glass, organic synthesis
compounds.funFact: Potassium fluoride was historically used in etching glass to create frosted patterns. Pure fluorine gas is made by electrolysing molten potassium fluoride!
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — potassium fluoride is highly toxic and causes the same type of injury as hydrofluoric acid!

compounds.realWorldUse: Anti-reflective coating on camera lenses and glasses
compounds.funFact: The thin blue coating you see on camera and spectacle lenses is often magnesium fluoride! It reduces glare by over 99% through destructive interference.

compounds.realWorldUse: Making glass, ceramics, rat poison
compounds.funFact: Barium carbonate is used in ceramic glazes but is also a rat poison! It decomposes in the stomach to form toxic barium ions that disrupt heart rhythm.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — barium carbonate is toxic and disrupts heart rhythm!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making aluminium compounds, leather tanning, antiperspirant
compounds.funFact: Aluminium nitrate is unusual because it decomposes on heating to give aluminium oxide, nitrogen dioxide and oxygen — three different products at once!

compounds.realWorldUse: Etching circuit boards, water treatment, photography
compounds.funFact: Ferric chloride etches copper — it's the chemical that creates the microscopic tracks on every printed circuit board in every electronic device!

compounds.realWorldUse: Salt and vinegar crisp flavouring, hand warmers, food preservative
compounds.funFact: Sodium acetate hand warmers work by supercooling — liquid sodium acetate stays liquid below its freezing point until you click a metal disc, triggering instant crystallisation and heat!

compounds.realWorldUse: De-icing airport runways, food preservative
compounds.funFact: Potassium acetate is the de-icer sprayed on airport runways and aircraft! Unlike road salt, it's biodegradable and safe for the environment.

compounds.realWorldUse: Optics for UV and IR light, nuclear reactor coolant
compounds.funFact: Lithium fluoride transmits ultraviolet light better than almost any other material — used in special optics for UV lasers and astronomical instruments!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making hydrogen peroxide, bleaching agent, igniter for thermite
compounds.funFact: Barium peroxide was the original industrial method for making hydrogen peroxide! It reacts with water to release oxygen, used to ignite thermite in some military applications.
compounds.dangerWarning: DO NOT MAKE THIS — barium peroxide is a toxic oxidiser that can ignite combustible materials!

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertiliser micronutrient, making dry cell batteries, animal feed supplement
compounds.funFact: Manganese is essential for plant growth — manganese sulfate cures the yellowing leaves caused by manganese deficiency in plants!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making cobalt oxide for batteries, blue pigment for glass
compounds.funFact: Cobalt nitrate solution soaked into paper then heated creates 'sympathetic ink' — invisible until heated, then turning brilliant blue!

compounds.realWorldUse: Making nickel oxide for fuel cells, nickel plating
compounds.funFact: Nickel nitrate is used to make the nickel oxide electrodes in some fuel cells — including those that might power future electric vehicles!

compounds.realWorldUse: Chrome plating, mordant for textile dyeing, catalyst
compounds.funFact: Chromium nitrate gives chrome plating its brilliant mirror finish. Chromium is named after the Greek word for colour because its compounds are so vividly colourful!

compounds.realWorldUse: Smelling salts, old-fashioned baking powder, fire extinguisher
compounds.funFact: Ammonium carbonate was used as the original smelling salts to revive people who had fainted! The sharp ammonia smell is unmistakable and instantly alerting.

compounds.realWorldUse: Fertiliser, fire retardant, food additive
compounds.funFact: Ammonium phosphate is one of the most widely used fertilisers globally, providing both nitrogen and phosphorus. It's also sprayed from aircraft to fight forest fires!
